content:
Noam Shazeer co-wrote the Transformer paper. Google paid billions to get him back. He just joined OpenAI anyway

3 min read
Just now

--

There is a pattern in Noam Shazeer’s career that is worth paying attention to. He co-authored “Attention Is All You Need” at Google in 2017. Left to build Character.AI. Google paid $2.7 billion to bring him back in 2024. On June 18, 2026, he announced he was joining OpenAI as Lead for Architecture Research.
The pattern is simple: Shazeer builds the foundations that the entire industry runs on, and no amount of money keeps him in one place for long.
What He Actually Built
The 2017 paper introduced the Transformer architecture. Before it, sequence models were dominated by RNNs and LSTMs. They were slow to train, hard to parallelize, and struggled with long-range dependencies.
Transformers solved all three problems at once with self-attention. The architecture allowed models to process entire sequences in parallel and to directly model relationships between any two tokens, regardless of distance.
GPT runs on it. Gemini runs on it. Claude runs on it. The entire modern AI stack traces back to that paper and its eight authors. Shazeer was one of them.
After leaving Google, he built Character.AI around a different idea: that the most interesting AI applications are social and emotional, not just informational. The company grew fast. By 2024, it was processing billions of messages per month.
The $2.7 Billion Acquisition
In August 2024, Google closed what was structured as a licensing deal but functioned as an acqui-hire. $2.7 billion for the team and the technology, with Shazeer returning to Google DeepMind as co-lead of Gemini.
The number was not arbitrary. Google needed someone who understood attention mechanisms and mixture-of-experts architecture at a level that very few people in the world do. Shazeer was the most credible option available.
Mixture-of-experts, or MoE, is the other area where his work matters. Standard dense models activate all parameters for every input. MoE models activate only a subset of specialized sub-networks per request. The result is that you can scale total parameter count — and thus model capacity — without proportionally scaling compute costs.
Both GPT-4 and Gemini are widely believed to use MoE architectures. Shazeer’s fingerprints are on the approach.
Why He Left Anyway
The honest answer is that Google could not offer what OpenAI offered.
Not in salary — Google can match any number. But Shazeer’s career suggests he is motivated by the specific problem, not the compensation. At Google DeepMind, he was a senior technical lead on an existing product. At OpenAI, he is Lead for Architecture Research — the person responsible for deciding how the next generation of models will be structured.
There is a version of that difference that no buyout can close.
What This Actually Changes
In the short term: nothing. Gemini stays Gemini. GPT-4o stays GPT-4o.
But architectural decisions made today show up in model capabilities 18 to 24 months from now. Shazeer’s arrival at OpenAI means the next significant structural changes in LLMs are more likely to come from GPT than from Gemini.
That is not a prediction about which model will be better. It is an observation about where the relevant expertise just moved.
The Broader Point
The AI race is often discussed in terms of compute, data, and capital. Those matter. But the history of the field keeps returning to a smaller number of people who make the architectural decisions that everyone else builds on.
Shazeer is one of those people. Google spent $2.7 billion on that fact and held it for 18 months.
Now OpenAI has it.
Sources: mlq.ai, techtimes.com, theaiinsider.tech
Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, OpenAI, Google, Technology
What does the AI talent market look like when $2.7 billion buys you 18 months?
